label: Biology
cards: They generate ATP, the cell's energy currency, and carry their own small genome inherited from the mother.
The molecule that carries genetic instructions
Its double-helix structure was described by Watson and Crick in 1953, building on Rosalind Franklin's imaging.
The process plants use to make food from sunlight
It converts carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen, using chlorophyll to capture light.
The number of chromosomes in a typical human cell
Arranged as 23 pairs; one of each pair comes from each parent.
The largest organ of the human body
It regulates temperature, blocks pathogens, and in an adult covers about two square metres.
The scientist who proposed evolution by natural selection
His 1859 book On the Origin of Species laid out the mechanism after his voyage on HMS Beagle.
The blood cells that carry oxygen
They use haemoglobin, an iron-rich protein that binds oxygen and gives blood its colour.
The pigment that makes plants green
It absorbs red and blue light and reflects green, which is why leaves look green to us.
The kingdom to which mushrooms belong
Neither plant nor animal, fungi absorb nutrients externally and are more closely related to animals than plants.
The study of heredity and genes
Its foundations were laid by Gregor Mendel's experiments with pea plants in the 1860s.
The part of the brain controlling balance and coordination
It sits at the back of the skull and fine-tunes movement, though it holds over half the brain's neurons.
The gas humans exhale that plants take in
This exchange is the basis of the carbon cycle linking animals and plants.
The process by which a cell divides into two identical cells
It underlies growth and tissue repair; sex cells instead form through meiosis.
The cell theory holds that all living things are made of cells and all cells come from existing cells.
The human bone that is the longest
The thigh bone bears most of the body's weight and is stronger than concrete by weight.
The system that includes the heart and blood vessels
It delivers oxygen and nutrients and removes waste, pumping blood through roughly 100,000 km of vessels.
The name for animals that eat only plants
Contrast with carnivores (meat) and omnivores (both), like humans.
The double-stranded shape of the DNA molecule
Two strands twist around each other, linked by base pairs like rungs of a ladder.

label: Chemistry
cards: From the Latin aurum, meaning shining dawn. Gold's symbol is why 'Au' appears on jewellery marks.
From the Latin ferrum. Iron is the most common element on Earth by mass.
The most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere
It makes up about 78% of the air; oxygen is second at about 21%.
The pH value that is exactly neutral
Below 7 is acidic, above 7 is alkaline. Pure water sits at 7.
The lightest element in the periodic table
It has a single proton and is the most abundant element in the universe.
Two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom.
Made of pure carbon in a rigid lattice; the same element as graphite in pencils, arranged differently.
The number of elements in the modern periodic table (approx.)
Elements up to atomic number 118 (oganesson) have been confirmed.
The scientist who created the periodic table
He arranged elements by atomic weight in 1869 and left gaps predicting undiscovered ones.
The process of a solid turning directly into a gas
Dry ice (solid carbon dioxide) does this at room temperature, skipping the liquid stage.
From the Latin natrium. Sodium reacts violently with water.
The gas that makes soft drinks fizzy
It is dissolved under pressure and escapes as bubbles when the bottle is opened.
The negatively charged particle in an atom
It orbits the nucleus and its movement between atoms is what electricity fundamentally is.
The acid found in the human stomach
It helps digest food and kill bacteria, and is strong enough to dissolve metal.
The most abundant metal in the Earth's crust
Though common, it is rarely found pure and was once more valuable than gold.
Essential for respiration and combustion; it makes up about 21% of the atmosphere.
The type of bond where atoms share electrons
Contrast with ionic bonds, where electrons are transferred rather than shared.
The centre of an atom containing protons and neutrons
Tiny but dense, it holds almost all of an atom's mass.

label: Physics & Space
cards: The speed of light in a vacuum (approx., km/s)
Precisely 299,792 km/s. Nothing with mass can reach it.
The force that pulls objects toward Earth
Newton described it; Einstein reframed it as the curving of spacetime by mass.
The closest planet to the Sun
It has no atmosphere to trap heat, so it swings between scorching days and freezing nights.
The largest planet in the solar system
A gas giant so large that all other planets could fit inside it, with a storm bigger than Earth.
The star at the centre of our solar system
A middle-aged star that accounts for over 99% of the solar system's mass.
A barred spiral galaxy holding hundreds of billions of stars; we sit on one of its arms.
The planet known as the Red Planet
Its colour comes from iron oxide, essentially rust, on its surface.
The scientist who developed the theory of relativity
His 1905 and 1915 papers reshaped our understanding of space, time, and gravity.
The force resisting motion between two surfaces
It converts kinetic energy into heat, which is why rubbing your hands warms them.
Named after Georg Ohm; his law relates voltage, current, and resistance.
The first person to walk on the Moon
In July 1969 during Apollo 11, followed minutes later by Buzz Aldrin.
The invisible line around which the Earth spins
Its tilt of about 23.5 degrees is what gives us the seasons.
The phenomenon of light bending as it passes through water
It is why a straw looks bent in a glass and how lenses focus light.
The planet famous for its prominent rings
Its rings are made mostly of ice and rock; other gas giants have faint rings too.
The energy an object has due to its motion
It depends on mass and the square of speed, so doubling speed quadruples it.
The point around which a lever pivots
Moving the fulcrum closer to a load lets a small force lift a heavy weight.
The natural satellite of the Earth
It drives the tides and is slowly drifting away from Earth by a few centimetres a year.
The three states of matter taught in basic science
Plasma is often called the fourth state, common in stars and lightning.

label: World Geography
cards: The longest river in the world
It flows north through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 km, though the Amazon rivals it in length.
It covers about a third of the planet's surface, larger than all land combined.
The tallest mountain above sea level
It rises about 8,849 m in the Himalayas; measured from base, Mauna Kea is taller.
The largest desert in the world
A desert is defined by low precipitation, not heat, so the frozen Antarctic qualifies as the largest.
The largest country by land area
It spans eleven time zones and about an eighth of Earth's inhabited land.
The continent that is also a country
It is the only landmass that is simultaneously a continent, a country, and an island.
The largest hot desert in the world
It stretches across North Africa, roughly the size of the United States.
The sea between Europe and Africa
Its name means 'middle of the earth' in Latin; it links to the Atlantic at Gibraltar.
The largest island in the world
Australia is larger but is classed as a continent, leaving Greenland the largest island.
The river that flows through the Amazon rainforest
It carries more water than the next several largest rivers combined.
The highest waterfall in the world
It drops nearly a kilometre in Venezuela, so high the water often mists before reaching the bottom.
The mountain range separating Europe and Asia
This modest range is the traditional dividing line between the two continents.
The largest freshwater lake by surface area
One of North America's Great Lakes, it could cover both of the Americas in a foot of water.
The imaginary line at zero degrees latitude
It divides the globe into Northern and Southern hemispheres and is where days and nights are most equal.
The strait separating Europe from Africa at their closest point
Only about 14 km wide, it connects the Mediterranean to the Atlantic.
By land area it is the smallest of the seven traditional continents.
The deepest point in Earth's oceans
Its Challenger Deep plunges nearly 11 km, deeper than Everest is tall.
The line of longitude marking zero degrees
It passes through Greenwich, London, and sets the basis for world time zones.

label: World History
cards: The ancient wonder still standing in Giza
The Great Pyramid is the only Ancient Wonder of the World largely intact today.
The deadliest conflict in history, it reshaped borders and led to the founding of the UN.
The ship that sank on its maiden voyage in 1912
Believed unsinkable, it struck an iceberg in the North Atlantic with great loss of life.
The wall that divided a German city until 1989
Its fall became a symbol of the end of the Cold War and the reunification of Germany.
At its height it circled the Mediterranean and shaped law, language, and architecture for millennia.
The explorer who reached the Americas in 1492
Sailing for Spain, he opened lasting contact between Europe and the Americas.
The Egyptian queen famed for alliances with Rome
The last active ruler of Ptolemaic Egypt, allied with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony.
The period of rebirth in art and learning in Europe
Beginning in Italy around the 14th century, it revived classical ideas and produced masters like Leonardo.
The document sealed at Runnymede in 1215
It limited the English king's power and influenced later ideas of rights and law.
The Soviet cosmonaut orbited Earth once in 1961, opening the era of human spaceflight.
The ancient Greek city known for its warriors
Its society was built around military training, contrasting with Athens's focus on philosophy and art.
The plague that devastated Europe in the 14th century
It killed perhaps a third of Europe's population in a few years.
The revolution that began in France in 1789
It overthrew the monarchy and spread ideas of liberty, equality, and fraternity.
The wall built across northern Britain by Rome
It marked the northwest frontier of the Roman Empire for nearly three centuries.
The general who crossed the Alps with elephants
The Carthaginian commander stunned Rome by invading Italy from the north around 218 BC.
The civilisation that built Machu Picchu
The Inca created a vast Andean empire linked by stone roads, without the wheel or iron tools.
The cold conflict between the US and USSR
A decades-long standoff of ideology, arms, and proxy wars without direct large-scale fighting.
The mound tomb complex of the first emperor of China
Thousands of life-sized clay soldiers were buried to guard Qin Shi Huang in the afterlife.

label: Art & Culture
cards: The artist who painted the Mona Lisa
A Renaissance polymath, he left the small portrait unfinished-looking yet endlessly studied.
The artist who cut off part of his own ear
The Dutch painter sold almost nothing in life but became one of history's most beloved artists.
The number of colours in a rainbow, traditionally
Red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet, a division popularised by Newton.
The Italian city that is the home of the Renaissance
Backed by the Medici family, it nurtured Michelangelo, Botticelli, and Leonardo.
The ceiling painted by Michelangelo in the Vatican
He spent about four years painting its frescoes, including The Creation of Adam.
The sculptor of the statue of David
He carved the towering marble figure from a single flawed block others had abandoned.
Its keys span black and white notes across seven octaves plus a few extra.
The art style of Picasso featuring fragmented shapes
He and Georges Braque pioneered it, showing subjects from many viewpoints at once.
The number of lines in a sonnet
A fixed poetic form; Shakespeare's sonnets follow a strict rhyme scheme.
The dance and music style born in Argentina
It emerged in the Rio de la Plata region and became a global ballroom dance.
The painting technique using small dots of colour
Seurat pioneered it; up close it is dots, from afar the colours blend in the eye.
The family of instruments including violin and cello
They produce sound from vibrating strings, bowed or plucked.
The Dutch painter of Girl with a Pearl Earring
A master of light, he left only around 34 known paintings.
The term for a play with a sad or serious ending
One of the classical Greek dramatic forms, contrasted with comedy.
Mixing these traditional primaries yields the other colours of the artist's palette.
The Norwegian painter of The Scream
Its swirling sky and anguished figure became an icon of modern anxiety.

label: Literature & Language
cards: The playwright who wrote Romeo and Juliet
The English playwright wrote some 37 plays and coined hundreds of still-used phrases.
The most widely spoken language by native speakers
Over a billion people speak it as a first language.
The author of the Harry Potter series
She wrote the first book partly in cafes and reshaped children's publishing.
The Greek poet credited with the Iliad and Odyssey
These ancient epics of the Trojan War and its aftermath anchor Western literature.
The detective created by Arthur Conan Doyle
Famed for deductive reasoning and his address at 221B Baker Street.
The number of plays in a typical trilogy
From the Greek for a set of three related works.
The author of Pride and Prejudice
Her sharp social novels of Regency England remain widely read and adapted.
The language from which most Romance languages descend
Spanish, French, Italian, Portuguese, and Romanian all evolved from spoken Latin.
The Russian author of War and Peace
His sweeping novel follows Russian society through the Napoleonic Wars.
Traditionally 5-7-5 syllables, often evoking nature and a single moment.
The author of the Lord of the Rings
A philologist, he invented entire languages before writing the stories around them.
The study of the origin of words
It traces how words change in form and meaning across languages and centuries.
The English poet who wrote Paradise Lost
He composed the epic while blind, dictating it to assistants.
The figure of speech comparing using 'like' or 'as'
'Brave as a lion' is a simile; drop the 'as' and 'he is a lion' becomes a metaphor.
The alphabet used to write English
Also called the Roman alphabet, it is the world's most widely used writing system.
The ancient library that was a wonder of learning
In ancient Egypt it aimed to collect all the world's knowledge before its decline.

label: Sport
cards: The oldest tennis tournament in the world, played on grass courts in London since 1877.
The number of players on a football (soccer) team on the pitch
Each side fields ten outfield players and one goalkeeper.
The sport associated with the Tour de France
A multi-week road race, its overall leader wears the famous yellow jersey.
The event held every four years for many sports
Revived in 1896, the modern Games trace back to ancient Greece.
The number of rings on the Olympic flag
Their interlocking design represents the five inhabited continents united in sport.
The board game of kings and queens with 64 squares
A game of pure strategy with origins in India over a thousand years ago.
The sport where you score a 'try'
Grounding the ball over the opponent's line scores a try, the sport's main way to score.
The number of holes in a full round of golf
A standard course is played in two loops of nine.
The martial art meaning 'gentle way' in Japanese
It emphasises throws and holds over strikes and is an Olympic sport.
Potting every ball in the ideal sequence yields this perfect score.
The number of players on a basketball team on court
Each team has five players; games are decided by scoring baskets worth two or three points.
The distance of a marathon (approx., km)
Precisely 42.195 km, based on a legendary run from Marathon to Athens.
The sport combining swimming, cycling, and running
Athletes complete all three back to back without stopping the clock.
The team sport played on ice with a puck
Fast and physical, players use sticks to shoot a rubber puck into the net.
The number of points for a standard goal in football (soccer)
Every goal counts the same, which is part of why low scores can still be dramatic.

label: Music
cards: The number of strings on a standard guitar
Tuned E-A-D-G-B-E from lowest to highest in standard tuning.
The composer of the Ninth Symphony who was deaf
He composed some of his greatest works after losing his hearing.
The Italian word for 'loud' in music
Its opposite is piano, meaning soft, which also gave the pianoforte its name.
The family of instruments including trumpet and trombone
Sound is made by buzzing the lips into a mouthpiece and changing the tubing length.
The number of notes in a standard major scale
Eight if you count the octave, the repeat of the first note higher up.
The Austrian child prodigy composer Wolfgang Amadeus
He was composing and performing across Europe before the age of ten.
The written symbols that represent musical sounds
Placed on a staff of five lines, their position sets pitch and their shape sets duration.
The tempo marking meaning 'fast and lively'
One of many Italian tempo terms, sitting between moderato and presto.
The keyboard instrument found in churches with pipes
Air pushed through pipes of different sizes produces its powerful range of sounds.
The German composer of the Brandenburg Concertos
A master of the Baroque era, revered for his intricate counterpoint.
The five lines on which music is written
Notes above and below the five lines extend the range using short ledger lines.
The sign that raises a note by a semitone
Its opposite, the flat, lowers a note by the same small step.

label: Film
cards: The film studio with a roaring lion logo
Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer's mascot Leo the Lion has opened its films for a century.
The genre of films meant to frighten the audience
It builds dread and fear, from ghost stories to monsters to psychological terror.
The animation studio behind Toy Story
Toy Story was the first fully computer-animated feature film.
The director of Jaws and Jurassic Park
One of the most commercially successful directors in cinema history.
The term for the person who directs actors in a film
They shape the creative vision, guiding performances, camera, and editing.
The film award shaped like a golden statuette
Officially the Academy Award, its gold-plated figure is a Hollywood icon.
The type of film that documents real life
It presents factual subjects rather than fictional stories.
The first feature-length film with synchronised sound
Its 1927 release helped end the silent film era.
The genre of Star Wars and Star Trek
It explores imagined futures, technology, and space.
The role of the person who writes a film's script
They craft the dialogue and structure before filming begins.

label: General Knowledge
cards: The number of continents on Earth
Asia, Africa, North America, South America, Antarctica, Europe, and Australia.
The number of days in a leap year
An extra day, 29 February, is added roughly every four years to match Earth's orbit.
The number of sides on a hexagon
From the Greek 'hex' for six; honeycombs use hexagons for efficient packing.
The number of colours on a traffic light
Red, amber, and green, a system standardised worldwide for road safety.
It can exceed 30 metres and is the largest animal ever known to have lived.
It can sprint to around 100 km/h in short bursts to catch prey.
The number of legs on a spider
Eight legs distinguish arachnids from insects, which have six.
The hardest substance in the human body
It coats the teeth and is even harder than bone, though it cannot repair itself.
The number of strings on a violin
Tuned in fifths: G, D, A, E from lowest to highest.
The metal that is liquid at room temperature
The only metal liquid at ordinary temperatures, once used in thermometers.
The number of planets in the solar system
Since Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006, eight planets remain.
The tallest animal in the world
Its long neck, with the same number of bones as a human's, helps it reach high leaves.
The number of degrees in a circle
A convention inherited from ancient Babylonian mathematics based on 60.
The number of squares on a chessboard
An eight-by-eight grid of alternating light and dark squares.
The only bird that can fly backwards
Its wings beat in a figure-eight, letting it hover and reverse mid-air.
The largest species of big cat
Larger than lions, the biggest tigers can weigh over 300 kg.
The gas that makes up most of the Sun
The Sun fuses hydrogen into helium, releasing the energy that lights the solar system.
The number of bones in the adult human body
Babies are born with about 300, many of which fuse together as they grow.

label: Mathematics
cards: The value of pi to two decimal places
The ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter, an endless non-repeating decimal.
Every other even number is divisible by 2, so 2 is uniquely both even and prime.
The result of any number multiplied by zero
A foundational rule: multiplying by nothing always gives nothing.
The number of degrees in a right angle
Two right angles make a straight line of 180 degrees.
The name for a triangle with all sides equal
All three angles are also equal, each measuring 60 degrees.
The next number in the Fibonacci sequence after 5, 8
Each number is the sum of the two before it: 5 plus 8 makes 13.
The number of zeros in one million
One million is written as a 1 followed by six zeros.
The branch of maths dealing with shapes and space
From the Greek for 'earth measurement', it underlies architecture and navigation.
The name for numbers less than zero
They sit left of zero on the number line and are vital for debts and temperatures.
The longest side of a right-angled triangle
Opposite the right angle, its length links to the others via the Pythagorean theorem.

label: Food & Nature
cards: The spice derived from crocus flowers, the most expensive
Each flower yields only a few threads, so it is harvested by hand at great cost.
The fruit that keeps the doctor away, by the saying
'An apple a day' reflects its fibre and vitamins, though any fruit helps.
The process bees use to make honey from nectar
While gathering nectar, bees transfer pollen, fertilising the plants they visit.
A single mature oak can drop thousands of acorns in a good year.
Technically a berry, it is prized for its creamy, fatty flesh.
The hardest working insect, symbol of industry
A hive's workers may visit millions of flowers to make a single jar of honey.
The bean used to make chocolate
Cocoa beans are fermented, roasted, and ground to make chocolate.
The largest type of nut, actually a seed, from a palm
Botanically a drupe, it can float across oceans to grow on new shores.
The green pigment process that feeds most life on Earth
It sits at the base of nearly every food chain by turning sunlight into food.
The grain that is the staple food for half the world
It feeds billions daily and grows in flooded paddies across Asia and beyond.
The fruit used to make wine
Crushed and fermented, grapes have been made into wine for thousands of years.
The fungus used to make bread rise
It ferments sugars into carbon dioxide, whose bubbles make dough expand.

label: The Human Body
cards: The organ that pumps blood around the body
About the size of a fist, it beats roughly 100,000 times a day.
The number of lungs in the human body
The left lung is slightly smaller to make room for the heart.
It makes up over half of blood's volume and carries cells and nutrients.
The organ responsible for filtering blood and making urine
The two kidneys filter the body's entire blood supply many times a day.
The largest muscle in the human body
The main muscle of the buttocks, it powers standing and climbing.
The tube connecting the mouth to the stomach
Muscle waves push food down it, so you can even swallow upside down.
The number of chambers in the human heart
Two atria on top and two ventricles below, keeping oxygen-rich and oxygen-poor blood apart.
The sense organ that detects light
It focuses light onto the retina, which sends signals to the brain to form images.
The vitamin the skin makes from sunlight
It helps the body absorb calcium for strong bones.
The part of the eye that controls how much light enters
The coloured iris around it widens or narrows the pupil like a camera aperture.
The system that defends the body against disease
It recognises and attacks invaders like bacteria and viruses.
The number of teeth in a full set of adult teeth
Including four wisdom teeth, which many people have removed.

label: Inventions & Technology
cards: The inventor associated with the practical light bulb
He refined a long-lasting design and built systems to power it commercially.
The company that makes the iPhone
Introduced in 2007, the iPhone helped define the modern smartphone.
The 'www' that begins many web addresses stands for this
Invented by Tim Berners-Lee, it made the internet easy to browse with links.
The machine invented by Gutenberg that spread books
Around 1440 it made books affordable and helped spark mass literacy in Europe.
Early versions used mercury or alcohol; digital sensors are now common.
The name for a program that browses the web
It fetches and displays web pages, translating code into what you see.
The two brothers credited with the first powered flight
Orville and Wilbur flew their aircraft in 1903, lasting only seconds.
The unit of digital information equal to eight bits
A single character of text is roughly one byte; a bit is the smallest unit.
The Italian who pioneered long-distance radio
He sent the first transatlantic radio signal in 1901.
The 'brain' of a computer that processes instructions
The central processing unit carries out the calculations that run software.
The technology that lets phones connect without wires
Radio waves carry data through the air between devices and networks.
The code of ones and zeros computers use
Every piece of digital data reduces to combinations of just two digits.

label: Mythology
cards: The Greek king of the gods on Mount Olympus
God of sky and thunder, he ruled the Olympians; the Romans called him Jupiter.
The Greek god of the sea
Wielding a trident, he stirred storms and earthquakes; his Roman name is Neptune.
The winged horse of Greek myth
Born from the blood of Medusa, it carried heroes and became a constellation.
The Greek hero known for his vulnerable heel
Invincible but for his heel, he was central to the Trojan War.
The Norse god of thunder with a hammer
His hammer Mjolnir summoned lightning; Thursday is named after him.
The one-eyed giants of Greek myth
These giant smiths forged Zeus's thunderbolts in some tales.
Goddess of love and beauty, her Greek counterpart is Aphrodite.
The Greek hero who completed twelve labours
Famed for superhuman strength, he performed feats like slaying the Nemean lion.
The creature with a lion's body and human head guarding riddles
In Greek myth it devoured those who could not answer its riddle.
The Norse realm of the gods
Home of Odin and Thor, linked to the mortal world by a rainbow bridge.
The underworld ruled by Hades in Greek myth
The realm of the dead, guarded by the three-headed dog Cerberus.
The maze-dwelling bull-headed monster of Crete
Kept in a labyrinth, it was slain by the hero Theseus.

label: World & Society
cards: The currency used across much of Europe
Adopted by many EU countries, it is one of the world's most traded currencies.
The international body founded in 1945 to promote peace
It succeeded the League of Nations and now has nearly 200 member states.
The document that lists universal human rights, adopted 1948
A landmark statement setting out rights everyone is entitled to.
The economic term for a general rise in prices
It reduces what a unit of money can buy over time.
The green movement concerned with protecting nature
It promotes sustainability and guarding ecosystems and species.
The system of government where people vote for leaders
From the Greek for 'rule by the people', it rests on free and fair elections.
The study of how societies produce and use resources
It examines choices under scarcity, from households to whole nations.
The colour associated with recycling bins in many places
Green widely symbolises nature and environmental care.
The three primary branches of many governments
Separating powers keeps any single branch from dominating.
The organisation known for its red cross emblem aiding disasters
It provides humanitarian aid in conflicts and disasters worldwide.

label: European capitals
cards: Paris is the seat of government of France.
Berlin is the seat of government of Germany.
Madrid is the seat of government of Spain.
Rome is the seat of government of Italy.
Zagreb is the seat of government of Croatia.
Vienna is the seat of government of Austria.
Budapest is the seat of government of Hungary.
Warsaw is the seat of government of Poland.
Athens is the seat of government of Greece.
Lisbon is the seat of government of Portugal.
Oslo is the seat of government of Norway.
Stockholm is the seat of government of Sweden.
Helsinki is the seat of government of Finland.
Copenhagen is the seat of government of Denmark.
Amsterdam is the seat of government of Netherlands.
Bern is the seat of government of Switzerland.
Prague is the seat of government of Czechia.
Dublin is the seat of government of Ireland.

label: Geography records
cards: The longest river in the world
About 6,650 km; the Amazon is the largest by volume.
The tallest mountain above sea level
The longest mountain range on land

label: Inventions and inventors
cards: Inventor of the world wide web
Inventor of movable-type printing in europe
Inventor of the telegraph code of dots and dashes

label: Roman numerals
cards: Roman numeral IV as a number
Roman numeral IX as a number
Roman numeral XIV as a number
Roman numeral XIX as a number
Roman numeral XL as a number
Roman numeral XC as a number
Roman numeral CD as a number
Roman numeral CM as a number
Roman numeral LXX as a number
Roman numeral MMXXVI as a number
Roman numeral XLVIII as a number
Roman numeral DCCC as a number

label: Official languages
cards: Main language of Kenya (official, besides English)

label: Greek alphabet order
cards: Greek letter: the letter after pi
Greek letter: the letter before pi
Greek letter: the letter after sigma
Greek letter: the letter after phi

label: Pivotal years
cards: Year of the first Moon landing
Year the printing press era began (Gutenberg Bible)
Year of the fall of Constantinople

label: Ancient world
cards: Civilization of the pyramids at Giza
City-state that fought Athens in the Peloponnesian War
Roman arena for gladiators in Rome
Greek philosopher who taught Alexander the Great
Wonder of the ancient world in Alexandria
River civilization of Mesopotamia lay between the Tigris and the...
Chinese defensive megastructure begun in antiquity

label: Painters of masterpieces
cards: Painter of Girl with a Pearl Earring
Painter of The Persistence of Memory (melting clocks)
Painter of The Birth of Venus

label: Books and their authors
cards: Author of The Old Man and the Sea
Author of One Hundred Years of Solitude

label: Norse mythology
cards: The world tree of Norse cosmology
The Norse end of the world

label: Space facts
cards: Largest planet in the Solar System
Force that keeps planets in orbit
A star that collapsed into an inescapable point
Telescope launched in 1990 that changed astronomy

label: Human body II
cards: Organ that filters blood into urine
Bones of the spine are called
Organ where digestion of food mainly begins after chewing
Number of chambers in the human heart

label: World geography II
cards: Canal linking the Mediterranean and Red Sea
Canal linking the Atlantic and Pacific
Mountain range between Europe and Asia
The sea with no coastline, bounded by currents
Desert covering much of Mongolia and northern China

label: Classical music
cards: Composer of the Ninth Symphony with Ode to Joy
Composer of Swan Lake and The Nutcracker
Composer of The Ride of the Valkyries
Polish composer of nocturnes for piano
Austrian composer nicknamed the father of the symphony

label: Computing basics
cards: A program that translates source code all at once
Repeating a block of code is called a
Data structure: last in, first out
Data structure: first in, first out
The father of computer science, of the machine and the test

label: Math facts
cards: The ratio of a circle’s circumference to its diameter
A number divisible only by 1 and itself
A triangle with all sides equal
The longest side of a right triangle
Angles of a triangle sum to (degrees)

label: Economics basics
cards: General rise of prices over time
Total value of goods and services produced (abbrev.)
Two consecutive quarters of shrinking GDP is called a
Money paid to shareholders from profits
When demand exceeds supply, prices tend to
The study of individual markets and actors
Government spending exceeding revenue creates a

label: Psychology basics
cards: Memory system you are using right now for this sentence
The tendency to seek confirming evidence
